title: Treasure Hunt Engine: Configuration Decisions That Actually Matter — Practical Configuration Guide
author: Veltrix
excerpt: Search data shows operators consistently hit this problem at the same stage of server growth. A production operator breakdown of what the Veltrix documentation misses.

content.text: Treasure Hunt Engine: Configuration Decisions That Actually Matter — Practical Configuration Guide   Category: Events
What Operators Are Actually Searching For

After a server is live, operators find they cannot guarantee Treasure Hunt Engine reward randomization because the events.yml mislabels map coordinates, leading to players losing clues. Operators seeking this Veltrix configuration topic typically uncover that the treasure coordinates in events.yml are being overwritten by another scheduled event, causing repeatable failures. This production problem—event file collision—drives the search. The engine references the prizes.yml where prizes are tied to integer IDs, but the IDs stay static between deployments until operators refresh them. When a new event starts, the older prize IDs persist, so no new loot looks different. Operators discover this problem only after the hunt fails repeatedly in the field, forcing them to dig into coordinators, timestamps, and config reload steps that are generally overlooked pre‑deployment.
Event Architecture and Why Structure Determines Participation

Veltrix’s event architecture is a lean framework that shapes how participants feel and act. In events.yml the event-type flag dictates whether a Treasure Hunt Engine (VX-EVT-01) or Capture the Flag (VX-EVT-02) runs, while max-participants bounds how many players can join in a single run. The time-limit parameter sets the race duration; setting it too short can cause frantic pacing, too long leads to disengagement.
Schedule Design: The Rhythm That Creates Login Habits

Spawn-location decides the initial loot clusters, and requires consistency to avoid surprise disadvantages. Prizes.yml feeds the reward system through prize-tier and reward-weight; misaligned weights create discontent when rewards feel unearned.

announce.yml’s announce-offset and announcement-message determine when the big notice appears; a 15‑second offset is too late for a 24‑hour event, while a 60‑second offset can scramble last‑minute strategy. Players who see clear, balanced limits, consistent spawn logic, and trustworthy reward distributions tend to stay, try multiple times, and invite friends, whereas uneven parameters create friction and eventual drop‑off.
The Mistakes That Compound Over Time

A common oversight in events.yml is setting the 'multiplier' to a uniform value across all events, which erases difficulty scaling and turns hard quests into trivial ones. Players quickly notice the absence of progressive rewards, leading to disengagement. Another frequent blunder is misconfiguring the 'cooldown' parameter of announce.yml; when set too low, chat floods with overlapping alerts, creating confusion and annoyance. Thirdly, many operators forget to enable 'share_rewards' in prizes.yml, causing only the winner to receive the prize pool while others see no incentive, which reduces participation. Finally, misusing the 'bracket_size' in tournament.yml by assigning a value larger than the available player pool generates empty slots, frustrating teams that expect a full bracket. Timing misalignments between events.yml start times and announce.yml triggers mean players either miss the kickoff or overlap with other activities, giving a chaotic experience.

The Parameters That Actually Control Behavior

Treasure Hunt Engine VX-EVT-01 relies on events.yml for shaping the gameplay. The most operative parameters, in descending priority, start with **event_enabled**, which must be toggled true during active windows; neglecting it disables all reward spawns. Next, **max_participants** decides how many players can join before a queue is forced—setting 20 for 50–100 players keeps the server from bursting, while a 200–player arena requires a higher 200 threshold to avoid holding players back. The **spawn_interval** governs how often loot blocks appear. Small camps of 25–40 users thrive with 10–12‑second intervals; a large flagship with 300 players needs faster 6–8 second cycles to sustain engagement. The **difficulty_level** parameter scales obstacle complexity—beginner or easy for casual rosters of 30, and expert for championship squads of 200+ to maintain challenge. Finally, **prize_pool** pulls from prizes.yml; modest private events use 25–30% of the pool; public tournaments consume up to 70%. Adjusting these ticks with player counts gives the treasure hunt a balanced, scalable feel.

When Your Server Starts to Scale

Treasure Hunt Engine VX-EVT-01 relies on events.yml for shaping the gameplay. The most operative parameters, in descending priority, start with event_enabled, which must be toggled true during active windows; neglecting it disables all reward spawns. Next, max_participants decides how many players can join before a queue is forced—setting 20 for 50–100 players keeps the server from bursting, while a 200–player arena requires a higher 200 threshold to avoid holding players back. The spawn_interval governs how often loot blocks appear. Small camps of 25–40 users thrive with 10–12 second intervals; a large flagship with 300 players needs faster 6–8 second cycles to sustain engagement. The difficulty_level parameter scales obstacle complexity—use beginner or easy for casual rosters of 30, and expert for championship squads of 200+ to maintain challenge. Finally, prize_pool pulls from prizes.yml; modest private events use 25–30% of the pool, while public tournaments consume up to 70%. By tuning these hot spots with player counts you ensure a balanced, scalable treasure hunt experience.
Operator Checklist Before You Go Live

Before the treasure hunt launches, the operator must verify that every Veltrix YAML file is in sync and logically consistent. The events.yml schedule block must not overlap with any other event’s start or end times, and its 'enabled' flag must be set to true. In prizes.yml the total monetary pool and each individual reward tier must sum to the announced value, and the 'wishlist' list must reference valid inventory identifiers. The announce.yml file should list all broadcast moments strictly after the event start and before the event end, and each entry’s delay must trigger at a positive timestamp, ensuring no immediate or negative broadcasts. For tournament.yml, the bracket depth and player capacity must match the treasure hunt’s participant limit; a mismatch should be flagged and prevented from enabling. Double‑check that all file paths and namespace references resolve to existing server sections; unresolved references will abort the event start sequence. Once every conditional statement in these YAML files returns true, the server can safely publish the treasure hunt to players.

The single most important rule for Veltrix operators is that all event YAML files stay in lockstep: a true flag in events.yml, a correct prize ledger, a scheduled announce window, and a bracket that matches capacity. Neglect any, and the event will fail.
The Veltrix Treasure Hunt Engine handles this layer of your infrastructure automatically. Pre-configured, documented, and ready to deploy on your server.
